perturbation.original_text: Invoices are payable thirty (30) days after receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Invoices are payable sixty (60) days after receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 60 days after receipt may violate Washington's prompt payment laws, which encourage shorter payment cycles to ensure timely compensation for goods and services.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts RCW 39.04.250, which concerns payment to contractors. While not directly applicable, extending payment terms significantly beyond typical business practices can be seen as an unfair delay, potentially leading to disputes and violating the spirit of prompt payment principles, especially if it causes undue hardship.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 39.04.250: Payments received on account of work performed by subcontractor—Disputed amounts—Remedies. Print RCWs > Title 39 > Chapter 39.04 > Section 39.04.250 39.04.240 << 39.04.250 >> 39.04.260 PDF RCW 39.04.250 Payments received on account of work performed by subcontractor — Disputed amounts — Remedies. (1) When payment is received by a contractor or subcontractor for work performed on a public work, the contractor or subcontractor shall pay to any subcontractor not later than ten days after the receipt of the payment, amounts allowed the contractor on account of the work performed by the subcontractor, to the extent of each subcontractor's interest therein. (2) In the event of a good faith dispute over all or any portion of the amount due on a payment from the state or a municipality to the prime contractor, or from the prime contractor or subcontractor to a subcontractor, then the state or the municipality, or the prime contractor or subcontractor, may withhold no more than one hundred fifty percent of the disputed amount. Those not a party to a dispute are entitled to full and prompt payment of their portion of a draw, progress payment, final payment, or released retainage. (3) In addition to all other remedies, any person from whom funds have been withheld in violation of this section shall be entitled to receive from the person wrongfully withholding the funds, for every month and portion thereof that payment including retainage is not made, interest at the highest rate allowed under RCW 19.52.025 . In any action for the collection of funds wrongfully withheld, the prevailing party shall be entitled to costs of suit and reasonable attorneys' fees. [ 1992 c 223 s 5 .] NOTES: Effective date — 1992 c 223: See note following RCW 39.76.011 . Waiver of rights, construction — Application — 1992 c 223: See RCW 39.04.900 and 39.04.901 .

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms from 30 to 60 days, which creates an inconsistency with prompt payment principles. While RCW 39.04.250 primarily addresses payments to subcontractors on public works projects, the Law Explanation suggests that significantly extending payment terms can be viewed as an unfair delay, violating the spirit of prompt payment principles, especially if it causes undue hardship. The original contract provides faster payment terms, while the modified contract introduces terms that may cause financial strain due to delayed payment. The definition for Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction says that time-sensitive obligations that do not align with legal requirements can create a legal contradiction. Extending payment from 30 to 60 days directly changes the timeframe for payment, and could be considered in violation of the prompt payment principles outlined in the Law Explanation. Therefore, the change creates a legally questionable inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: A five percent (5%) late fee will be added to all invoices which become past due and interest at a rate of twelve percent (12%) per annum will be charged on all balances not paid within thirty (30) days of the date they are due.
perturbation.changed_text: A twenty-five percent (25%) late fee will be added to all invoices which become past due and interest at a rate of twenty-five percent (25%) per annum will be charged on all balances not paid within thirty (30) days of the date they are due.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the late fee and interest rate to 25% may violate Washington's usury laws, which set limits on the maximum interest rates that can be charged on debts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates RCW 19.52.020, because the law states that no person shall directly or indirectly take or receive in money, goods, or things in action, or in any other way, any greater interest for the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action than twelve percent (12%) per annum.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.52.020: Highest rate permissible—Setup charges. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.52 > Section 19.52.020 19.52.010 << 19.52.020 >> 19.52.025 PDF RCW 19.52.020 Highest rate permissible — Setup charges. (1) Except as provided in subsection (4) of this section, any rate of interest shall be legal so long as the rate of interest does not exceed the higher of: (a) Twelve percent per annum; or (b) four percentage points above the equivalent coupon issue yield (as published by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System) of the average bill rate for twenty-six week treasury bills as determined at the first bill market auction conducted during the calendar month immediately preceding the later of (i) the establishment of the interest rate by written agreement of the parties to the contract, or (ii) any adjustment in the interest rate in the case of a written agreement permitting an adjustment in the interest rate. No person shall directly or indirectly take or receive in money, goods, or things in action, or in any other way, any greater interest for the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action. (2)(a) In any loan of money in which the funds advanced do not exceed the sum of five hundred dollars, a setup charge may be charged and collected by the lender, and such setup charge shall not be considered interest hereunder. (b) The setup charge shall not exceed four percent of the amount of funds advanced, or fifteen dollars, whichever is the lesser, except that on loans of under one hundred dollars a minimum not exceeding four dollars may be so charged. (3) Any loan made pursuant to a commitment to lend at an interest rate permitted at the time the commitment is made shall not be usurious. Credit extended pursuant to an open-end credit agreement upon which interest is computed on the basis of a balance or balances outstanding during a billing cycle shall not be usurious if on any one day during the billing cycle the rate at which interest is charged

perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the late fee and interest rate to 25%, while RCW 19.52.020 states that no person shall directly or indirectly take or receive any greater interest for the loan or forbearance of any money than twelve percent (12%) per annum, thus contradicting the law. Both scraped snippets directly support this law.

perturbation.original_text: The terms of this Paragraph shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement for whatever reason for a period of three (3) years after such expiration or termination.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms of this Paragraph shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement for whatever reason for a period of ten (10) years after such expiration or termination.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 10 years could be deemed unreasonably long, potentially conflicting with Washington state's statute of limitations for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: While RCW 4.16.040 doesn't explicitly prohibit confidentiality agreements lasting 10 years, it establishes a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract. An excessively long confidentiality period may create enforceability issues if a breach occurs near the end of the term and litigation is pursued. The court will consider the reasonableness of the duration when determining enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.16.040: Actions limited to six years. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.16 > Section 4.16.040 4.16.030 << 4.16.040 >> 4.16.050 PDF RCW 4.16.040 Actions limited to six years. The following actions shall be commenced within six years: (1) An action upon a contract in writing, or liability express or implied arising out of a written agreement, except as provided for in RCW 64.04.007 (2). (2) An action upon an account receivable. For purposes of this section, an account receivable is any obligation for payment incurred in the ordinary course of the claimant's business or profession, whether arising from one or more transactions and whether or not earned by performance. (3) An action for the rents and profits or for the use and occupation of real estate. [ 2012 c 185 s 3 ; 2007 c 124 s 1 ; 1989 c 38 s 1 ; 1980 c 105 s 2 ; 1927 c 137 s 1 ; Code 1881 s 27; 1854 p 363 s 3 ; RRS s 157.] NOTES: Application — 2007 c 124: "This act applies to all causes of action on accounts receivable, whether commenced before or after July 22, 2007." [ 2007 c 124 s 2 .] Application — 1980 c 105: See note following RCW 4.16.020 .

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period to 10 years, while RCW 4.16.040 establishes a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract. The LLM explanation says an excessively long confidentiality period may create enforceability issues if a breach occurs near the end of the term and litigation is pursued. The scraped law snippets (both Snippet 1 and Snippet 2) confirm that RCW 4.16.040 limits actions upon a contract to six years. Therefore, a ten-year confidentiality clause could be deemed unenforceable beyond the six-year statute of limitations, creating a legal contradiction because it sets a time frame that exceeds the legally permissible period for enforcement.
